EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system brought a swath of heavy snow and gusty winds as it tracked from central Illinois early Wednesday morning (3/5) through Wednesday evening. Snowfall totals across northern Wisconsin were generally 8 to 12 inches. Winds gusted to 35 to 45 mph Wednesday afternoon (3/5), leading to blowing snow.|��|Some of the highest snowfall totals included 13 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 11 inches near Phelps (Vilas Co.), 10.5 inches in Pelican Lake (Oneida Co.), 10.1 inches near Rhinelander (Oneida Co.) as well as near Mc Naughton (Oneida Co.), 9.9 inches in Argonne (Forest Co.), and 6.2 inches in Tomahawk (Lincoln Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 11 inches near Phelps, 8.5 inches near Conover, 7.7 inches in St. Germain, and 7.2 inches in Woodruff. Winds gusted to 36 to 43 mph at times Wednesday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across southern Minnesota the 28th into the 29th and brought rain and a few thunderstorms to the Northland. A few of the storms produced hail to the size of quarters during the afternoon with one report of half dollar sized hail in northern Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most were penny to nickel size and accumulated deep enough to shovel. The largest stones were quarter to half dollar sized.

An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains the morning of the 4th to the Wisconsin/Illinois border on the morning of the 5th. This put northwest Wisconsin on the northwest side of the low leading to heavy snowfall across the region. Widespread snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches were reported with higher amounts in Iron County to 8 to 12 inches. A high report of 15 inches was recorded in Gile. The snow was heavy and wet and led to damaged power lines in Burnett County along with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were reported across the county. A high report of 8.6 inches was received from Maple.

Low pressure moving through the Mid-Mississippi Valley brought a messy period of precipitation to northwestern Wisconsin on the 29th and into the 30th. Rain, freezing rain, and snow all occurred with this system and led to difficult travel conditions across the region. Ice accumulations of up to a quarter of an inch were reported in spots with snowfall of 3 to 8 inches. Some areas of northern Iron County in the higher elevations saw up to 10 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were in the 2 to 6 inch range with a high report of 7.5 inches in Mason.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex spring storm brought periods of freezing rain and light snow to northern and central Wisconsin from Saturday afternoon (3/29) through Sunday morning (3/30). Ice totals ranged from 0.25 to 0.60 inches with widespread tree damage and power outages as easterly winds gusted to 20 to 30 mph. More than 90,000 customers were without power across northeast Wisconsin during this event. Light snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were noted across portions of north-central Wisconsin with the first round of precipitation.||There was a lull in the precipitation later Sunday morning (3/30) into the early afternoon. The mixed precipitation changed over to all snow Sunday afternoon (3/30) and continued into the evening as winds became northerly and gusted to 20 to 30 mph. Snowfall amounts were generally 3 to 7 inches during the second round of snow across north-central Wisconsin, with the highest snowfall amounts including 7 inches in Florence (Florence Co.) and 6.7 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest ice total in Forest County was 0.50 inches in Argonne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the mid-Mississippi Valley to the central Great Lakes from Wednesday afternoon (3/19) through Wednesday night. A band of frontogenesis on the northwest side of the low produced heavy snow with northerly winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 9 inches in Athelstane (northern Marinette Co.) as well as Crivitz (southern Marinette Co.), 8.8 inches in Marshfield (Wood Co.), 8.7 inches in Hatley (Marathon Co.), 8.2 inches in Ephraim (Door Co.), 8 inches in Mountain (northern Oconto Co.) as well as Sister Bay (Door Co.) and Ellison Bay (Door Co.), and 7.7 inches in Stevens Point (Portage Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across southern Marinette County included 9 inches in Crivitz and 7 in Middle Inlet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colder air wrapped into southern Wisconsin as low pressure slowly exited through the Upper Great Lakes. Moisture and energy came together and lifted through southeast Wisconsin early Sunday morning. A narrow band of heavy snow from Milwaukee to Elkhorn, roughly 15 miles wide, dropped a swath of 4 to 6 inches of wet snow. Visibility was reduced to a quarter to eighth mile at times, and slushy accumulations caused slippery conditions on roads under the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulation of 3 to 6 inches over the southeast third of the county and much lower amounts in the northwest parts. Highest total of 6.2 inches observed in Muskego.

Ice totals ranged from 0.25 to 0.60 inches with widespread tree damage and power outages as easterly winds gusted to 20 to 30 mph. More than 90,000 customers were without power across northeast Wisconsin during this event. Light snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were noted across portions of north-central Wisconsin with the first round of precipitation.||There was a lull in the precipitation later Sunday morning (3/30) into the early afternoon. The mixed precipitation changed over to all snow Sunday afternoon (3/30) and continued into the evening as winds became northerly and gusted to 20 to 30 mph. Snowfall amounts were generally 3 to 7 inches during the second round of snow across north-central Wisconsin, with the highest snowfall amounts including 7 inches in Florence (Florence Co.) and 6.7 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain changed over to heavy snow during the afternoon with north northwest winds gusting to 20 to 30 mph. The highest snowfall total in Florence County was 7 inches in Florence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in Waupaca County was 7.5 inches south of Tigerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened as it tracked from the Great Plains to Minnesota. A broad line of storms spread into southwest Wisconsin during the late evening and produced damaging straight line winds across southern and east central Wisconsin into the early morning hours. There were numerous reports of wind damage across southern WI, along with measured wind gusts up to 75 mph. Winds were strongest along the leading edge of the line, though the strong winds persisted for up to a half hour. Trees and large branches were uprooted or snapped, with some falling on houses, cars and power lines. Some outbuildings sustained roof and siding damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station EW4463 1 mile east of Mazomanie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brisk northerly winds ushered colder air into central and southern Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening as strong low pressure tracked from IL into lower MI. A combination of 40-50 mph wind gusts and falling snow behind the cold front created hazardous conditions on roads across central to east central Wisconsin. Some sleet occurred as rain changed over to snow. Snowfall totals were in the 2 to 4 inch range north of I-94 and US Highway 14. The brisk north winds knocked down some trees in the Beloit and Milwaukee areas during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulations generally in the 2 to 4 inch range countywide. Highest total of 3.2 inches observed in Princeton. Peak wind gusts were around 40 mph during the evening while the snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colder air wrapped into southern Wisconsin as low pressure slowly exited through the Upper Great Lakes. Moisture and energy came together and lifted through southeast Wisconsin early Sunday morning. A narrow band of heavy snow from Milwaukee to Elkhorn, roughly 15 miles wide, dropped a swath of 4 to 6 inches of wet snow. Visibility was reduced to a quarter to eighth mile at times, and slushy accumulations caused slippery conditions on roads under the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulation of 3 to 6 inches over the northwest half of the county and much lower amounts in the southeast half. Highest total of 5.0 inches observed in Waterford.

Snowfall amounts were generally 3 to 7 inches during the second round of snow across north-central Wisconsin, with the highest snowfall amounts including 7 inches in Florence (Florence Co.) and 6.7 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest ice total in southern Oconto County was 0.30 inches in Lena as well as near Sampson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened as it tracked from the Great Plains to Minnesota. A broad line of storms spread into southwest Wisconsin during the late evening and produced damaging straight line winds across southern and east central Wisconsin into the early morning hours. There were numerous reports of wind damage across southern WI, along with measured wind gusts up to 75 mph. Winds were strongest along the leading edge of the line, though the strong winds persisted for up to a half hour. Trees and large branches were uprooted or snapped, with some falling on houses, cars and power lines. Some outbuildings sustained roof and siding damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of three uprooted trees and a toppled shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colder air wrapped into southern Wisconsin as low pressure slowly exited through the Upper Great Lakes. Moisture and energy came together and lifted through southeast Wisconsin early Sunday morning. A narrow band of heavy snow from Milwaukee to Elkhorn, roughly 15 miles wide, dropped a swath of 4 to 6 inches of wet snow. Visibility was reduced to a quarter to eighth mile at times, and slushy accumulations caused slippery conditions on roads under the heaviest snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulation of 3 to 6 inches over the south half of the county and much lower amounts in the north half. Highest total of 6.5 inches observed in Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass was located over the central CONUS the night of the 17th into the morning of the 18th. Air temperatures dropped into the teens and 20s below zero over much of the area under clear skies. Light winds were also observed with a pressure gradient overhead. This led to wind chills in the 30s and 40s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -37F were reported in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulations generally in the 2-4 inch range countywide. Highest total of 3.5 inches observed in Portage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total accumulations generally in the 3-5 inch range countywide. Highest total of 5.5 inches observed in West Bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong polar high pressure moved into the northern Great Plains late January 20 into January 21, allowing an arctic air mass to stream into the western Great Lakes. The overnight arrival of the air mass allowed low temperatures to fall to or below -10 degrees across the majority of southern Wisconsin by sunrise January 21. Breezy north winds combined with the frigid air temperatures to support widespread, extremely cold wind chills in the -30 to -40 degree range across most of southern Wisconsin. As a result of the extremely cold wind chills, virtually every southern Wisconsin school district canceled classes on January 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills in the -30 to -40 degree range were observed from 2 AM January 21 through the mid morning hours of January 21. Coldest wind chill of -38 was observed in Reedsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought a cold airmass over northeast Wisconsin from late Sunday night (1/19) through Tuesday morning (1/21). Air temperatures fell to 20 to 30 below zero late Sunday night (1/19) into Monday morning (1/20).  Light west winds of 5 to 10 mph allowed wind chills to fall to 35 to 43 below zero across much of north-central and portions of central Wisconsin.  ||Temperatures warmed during the day on Monday (1/20), with extreme cold temperatures returning Monday evening (1/20) and lasting through late morning on Tuesday (1/21). Air temperatures fell to 10 to 30 below zero Monday night (1/20) into Tuesday morning (1/21). West to northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph allowed wind chills to fall to 30 to nearly 50 below zero across all of northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chill values to fall to -30 to -34 during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked southeast from southern Alberta through North Dakota and continued into southern Wisconsin. This was a standard Clipper-type low with limited moisture. The primary accumulating snow started during the evening hours on January 11th, with most of the snow falling overnight into Sunday morning. The result was a swath of 1-4 inches of snow along I-94 in western Wisconsin. The highest snowfall amount was 3.8 inches in Frederic, WI (Polk).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of 2 to 4 inches fell across the county. 3.8 inches of snow was measured 2.5 miles SW of Frederic, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across the Upper Mississippi River Valley with several mornings of wind chills of 25 degrees below zero or colder from January 19th to the 22nd. The coldest period with this arctic airmass was the evening on the 20th through the morning hours on the 21st where widespread wind chills of 35 degrees below zero were observed across western Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills began on the evening of the 20th and continued through noon on the 21st in Clark County where values dropped between 25 to 45 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill in the county was 42 degrees below zero in Greenwood, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved through the Northland on the 11th into the 12th bringing snowfall to much of the region. The track of the system shifted a bit more to the south than expected and brought higher snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches across the northern parts of Douglas and Bayfield counties were some lake enhancement occurred. A high report of 9 inches was reported south of Cornucopia in northern Bayfield County with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals for the event were in the 6 to 8 inch range across the northern part of the county. The highest report was 9 inches about 4 miles south of Cornucopia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two bands of lake effect snow impacted far southeastern Wisconsin from the overnight hours of January 5 into the morning of January 6. The snow bands brought measurable snow to Milwaukee, Racine, and Kenosha Counties, with the highest totals focusing far northeastern Kenosha County and extreme southeastern Racine County. Areas of light freezing drizzle accompanied the second lake effect snow band, resulting in a glaze of ice accumulation on untreated roads and surfaces in Sheboygan, Ozaukee, Washington, and Waukesha Counties. Minor vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred as a result of the glaze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow bands bring generally 2-4 inches of snow accumulation to the eastern half of the county, with locally higher totals over far northeastern portions of the county. Highest accumulation of 6.0 inches observed on the far northeast side of the city of Kenosha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration extreme cold every took place across the region starting the 18th and lasting into the morning of the 21st. An area of very cold high pressure worked in the Plains on the 18th and 19th and eventually sunk to the south into the Southern Plains by the morning of the 21st. The Northland didn't see the coldest air temperatures of the event being more on the periphery of the high, but did see extremely cold wind chills as low as -54F as the pressure gradient overhead prevented winds from dying off overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -45F were reported at the KSUW Superior airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS located at the Red Wing Regional Airport recorded an extreme wind chill of -37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong polar high pressure moved into the northern Great Plains late January 20 into January 21, allowing an arctic air mass to stream into the western Great Lakes. The overnight arrival of the air mass allowed low temperatures to fall to or below -10 degrees across the majority of southern Wisconsin by sunrise January 21. Breezy north winds combined with the frigid air temperatures to support widespread, extremely cold wind chills in the -30 to -40 degree range across most of southern Wisconsin. As a result of the extremely cold wind chills, virtually every southern Wisconsin school district canceled classes on January 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills in the -30 to -40 degree range were observed from 2 AM January 21 through the mid morning hours of January 21. Coldest wind chill of -38 was observed in Lodi.

Air temperatures fell to 20 to 30 below zero late Sunday night (1/19) into Monday morning (1/20).  Light west winds of 5 to 10 mph allowed wind chills to fall to 35 to 43 below zero across much of north-central and portions of central Wisconsin.  ||Temperatures warmed during the day on Monday (1/20), with extreme cold temperatures returning Monday evening (1/20) and lasting through late morning on Tuesday (1/21). Air temperatures fell to 10 to 30 below zero Monday night (1/20) into Tuesday morning (1/21). West to northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph allowed wind chills to fall to 30 to nearly 50 below zero across all of northeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chill values to fall to -35 to -48 during the late evening and morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong polar high pressure moved into the northern Great Plains late January 20 into January 21, allowing an arctic air mass to stream into the western Great Lakes. The overnight arrival of the air mass allowed low temperatures to fall to or below -10 degrees across the majority of southern Wisconsin by sunrise January 21. Breezy north winds combined with the frigid air temperatures to support widespread, extremely cold wind chills in the -30 to -40 degree range across most of southern Wisconsin. As a result of the extremely cold wind chills, virtually every southern Wisconsin school district canceled classes on January 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills near and just below -30 degrees were observed from 4 AM January 21 through the mid morning hours of January 21. Coldest wind chill of -31 was observed in Cold Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across the Upper Mississippi River Valley with several mornings of wind chills of 25 degrees below zero or colder from January 19th to the 22nd. The coldest period with this arctic airmass was the evening on the 20th through the morning hours on the 21st where widespread wind chills of 35 degrees below zero were observed across western Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills began on the evening of the 20th and continued through noon on the 21st in Crawford County where values dropped to around 30 to 40 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill in the county was 40 degrees below zero in Steuben, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from northern Minnesota to eastern Lake Superior the 14th into the 15th. Light snow began during the afternoon of the 14th and was much fluffier than expected, allowing accumulations to be a bit higher than forecast. Some lake enhancement was also seen across northwest Wisconsin resulting in snowfall totals up to a foot in northern parts of Ashland and Bayfield counties. Otherwise, snowfall totals were generally around 6 inches or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across Price County was generally under 6 inches, but with the very fluffy nature of the snow and some slight lake enhancement, the far northern part of the county received 6 to 6.5 inches of snow in a few spots near Butternut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Ohio River Valley spread a swath of accumulating snow to southern Wisconsin from the morning through the late evening hours of February 12. Highest snowfall totals were across far south-central and southeastern Wisconsin, where numerous 6-8 inch accumulations were observed. Winter Storm Warnings & Winter Weather Advisories were issued in advance of the system, with school districts canceling classes and after school activities in the Winter Storm Warning area. Snowfall resulted in numerous accidents, leading to multiple brief closures of area interstates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total snow accumulations ranging generally between 1-3 inches. Highest total of 3.1 inches observed in Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass was located over the central CONUS the night of the 16th into the morning of the 17th. Air temperatures dropped into the teens and 20s below zero over much of the area under clear skies. Light winds were also observed with a pressure gradient overhead. This led to wind chills in the 30s and 40s below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -38F were reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts brought a frigid Canadian air mass and breezy northwest winds from the night of February 16 to the morning of February 19. This was the second stretch of dangerously cold air for the region this winter with temperatures into the -10s to -20s F, and wind chill temperatures between -25 and -40 F from late on the 16th through the morning of the 19th.||The coldest conditions persisted the longest for portions of extreme western WI, with New Richmond Regional Airport recording the lowest wind chill (-38F) on the morning of February 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from northern Minnesota to eastern Lake Superior the 14th into the 15th. Light snow began during the afternoon of the 14th and was much fluffier than expected, allowing accumulations to be a bit higher than forecast. Some lake enhancement was also seen across northwest Wisconsin resulting in snowfall totals up to a foot in northern parts of Ashland and Bayfield counties. Otherwise, snowfall totals were generally around 6 inches or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across Douglas County was generally under 6 inches, but with the very fluffy nature of the snow and some lake enhancement, upwards of 6 to 8 inches were reported across the northern part of the county for the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Enhanced snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches were reported across the county with the highest amounts across the northern part of the county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact but strong winter system moved through portions of the Upper Midwest on March 19th bringing heavy snow, strong wind gusts, and even thundersnow. While snow mixed in with sleet and rain at times, eventually some colder air aloft moved into the region and switched precipitation to all snow with some locally heavy rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour. A particularly intense band of heavy snow developed over the La Crosse area prompting local officials to declare a snow emergency. The highest snow totals in western Wisconsin occurred from La Crosse County through southern Clark County where 8 to 11 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter system brought heavy snow to Monroe County on March 19th. Snowfall totals ranged between 6 to 11 inches across the county with the highest reported total being 11.2 inches about 1 mile southwest of Tomah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of March, a powerful mid-latitude cyclone progressed through the Upper Midwest overnight on the 4th and into the morning and afternoon on the 5th. As this occurred, rain changed over to snow with increasing winds during the early morning hours on March 5th. Overall, snow accumulations across western Wisconsin were generally light, however gusty winds up to 35 mph led to some locally hazardous travel conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter system brought gusty winds and snow to La Crosse County on March 5th. Snowfall totals ranged around 1 to 2 inches across the county with the highest reported total being 2.3 inches at the NWS office in La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on March 14th, a line of thunderstorms moved through portions of northeast Iowa into southwest Wisconsin. While their presentation on radar was not overly impressive, numerous wind gusts of over 50 mph occurred including a wind gust to 59 mph in Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a wind gust to 59 mph near Union in Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall and upstream snowmelt towards the end of March contributed to a brief instance of minor flooding on the Black River at Black River Falls on March 31st. The river entered flood stage early in the morning on March 31st and quickly returned below flood stage later that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent rainfall and some snowmelt allowed the Black River to reach into minor flood stage just after midnight on March 31st. The river then crested by early morning at a stage of 47.94 ft in minor flood stage before falling below flood stage during the afternoon on March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The beginnings of a historic ice storm for the Great Lakes region impacted portions north-central Wisconsin at the end of March before moving east. During the morning of March 30th, law enforcement in both Clark and Taylor counties reported several instances of tree damage and trees falling onto county roads due to the significant ice accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 29th and 30th, a significant icing event occurred in Taylor County as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain resulted in substantial ice accumulations across the county. Law enforcement reported numerous instances of trees downed onto roads across Taylor County during morning hours on March 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 29th and 30th, a significant icing event occurred in Clark County as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain resulted in substantial ice accumulations across mainly the northern half of the county. Law enforcement reported numerous instances of tree damage across northern Clark County during morning hours on March 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened as it tracked from the Great Plains to Minnesota. A broad line of storms spread into southwest Wisconsin during the late evening and produced damaging straight line winds across southern and east central Wisconsin into the early morning hours. There were numerous reports of wind damage across southern WI, along with measured wind gusts up to 75 mph. Winds were strongest along the leading edge of the line, though the strong winds persisted for up to a half hour. Trees and large branches were uprooted or snapped, with some falling on houses, cars and power lines. Some outbuildings sustained roof and siding damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell through roof of a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the bigger tornado events for southern WI over the past 10-15 years with 2 EF-2s occurring and 8 tornadoes in total.  Juneau and Mayville took the brunt of the two strong tornadoes, while hundreds of large hail reports and several damaging wind reports were received across southern WI. A strong cold front, extending from strong low pressure over eastern North Dakota, combined with very unstable air and strong wind shear to bring widespread severe thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began southwest of Mayville just west of Highway 28 which was also captured on video as a potential multi-vortex tornado.  Some of the more intense damage occurred shortly after the tornado developed and crossed County Rd TW on into southwest Mayville in an industrial area.  A farmhouse on Cty RW lost a portion of its roof and some farm sheds. The tornado then moved through the industrial area in southwest Mayville.  The Mayville Engineering Company was hit on its northwest corner.  The winds from the tornado breached a large garage door on the west side which then pushed the large north end wall down.  The tornado then went through the Gleason Reel Corporation and destroyed the southern portion of the building.  It then went through a neighborhood near the St. John/Graceland cemetary before crossing Main St/Rock River.  The tornado then went through the east side of Mayville near the Mayville Golf Course.  It then continued to the northeast through mainly rural areas before ending NW of Theresa.

During the afternoon hours on May 15th, a developing complex of embedded supercell thunderstorms moved eastward into western Wisconsin. As they continued to intensify, they resulted in widespread hail of up to two inches in diameter across much of western Wisconsin and four tornadoes in Clark County. The strongest of these tornadoes was an EF-2 near Colby, Wisconsin where numerous power poles were downed and one home sustained significant damage to its roof and attached garage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported quarter sized hail in Centerville.

Juneau and Mayville took the brunt of the two strong tornadoes, while hundreds of large hail reports and several damaging wind reports were received across southern WI. A strong cold front, extending from strong low pressure over eastern North Dakota, combined with very unstable air and strong wind shear to bring widespread severe thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust at Batten International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on May 15th, a developing complex of embedded supercell thunderstorms moved eastward into western Wisconsin. As they continued to intensify, they resulted in widespread hail of up to two inches in diameter across much of western Wisconsin and four tornadoes in Clark County. The strongest of these tornadoes was an EF-2 near Colby, Wisconsin where numerous power poles were downed and one home sustained significant damage to its roof and attached garage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported quarter sized hail in Ithaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on May 15th, a developing complex of embedded supercell thunderstorms moved eastward into western Wisconsin. As they continued to intensify, they resulted in widespread hail of up to two inches in diameter across much of western Wisconsin and four tornadoes in Clark County. The strongest of these tornadoes was an EF-2 near Colby, Wisconsin where numerous power poles were downed and one home sustained significant damage to its roof and attached garage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This strong tornado developed in fields west of Unity before tracking northeast toward Colby. As it moved through rural areas of northeastern Clark County, it damaged multiple farm outbuildings, trees, numerous power poles, and a home. NWS Storm Survey indicated EF2 damage to this home as the majority of its roof was removed with the attached garage heavily damaged. The tornado continued into the southeastern edge of Colby where multiple outbuildings, a home, and a car dealership sustained damage. The tornado moved into Marathon County before it quickly dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on May 15th, a developing complex of embedded supercell thunderstorms moved eastward into western Wisconsin. As they continued to intensify, they resulted in widespread hail of up to two inches in diameter across much of western Wisconsin and four tornadoes in Clark County. The strongest of these tornadoes was an EF-2 near Colby, Wisconsin where numerous power poles were downed and one home sustained significant damage to its roof and attached garage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down southeast of Loyal and tracked to the east-northeast, damaging one residence in addition to trees and powerlines before quickly dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an occluded front Thursday afternoon (5/15) across western Wisconsin.  Storms tracked through the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The strongest storms produced 3 tornadoes, damaging winds, and large hail up to golf ball size across central and north-central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in fields west of Unity in Clark County (NWS La Crosse warning area) at 5:21 pm CDT before tracking northeast toward Colby. The tornado moved through rural areas of northeastern Clark County, damaging multiple farm outbuildings, trees, power poles, and a home before crossing Highway 13 into western Marathon County at 5:27 pm CDT.  The tornado tracked through the southeastern edge of Colby damaging another home, multiple outbuildings, power poles, and a car dealership before dissipating at 5:28 pm CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 4.81 miles long, with a width of 230 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the bigger tornado events for southern WI over the past 10-15 years with 2 EF-2s occurring and 8 tornadoes in total.  Juneau and Mayville took the brunt of the two strong tornadoes, while hundreds of large hail reports and several damaging wind reports were received across southern WI. A strong cold front, extending from strong low pressure over eastern North Dakota, combined with very unstable air and strong wind shear to bring widespread severe thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Track started southwest of Lowell and ended south of Juneau.  Damage was mainly to about 30 trees, but there was barn damage along Fairwood Rd near the onset of the tornado and some roofing panels lofted from the Animix facility south of Juneau near the end of the tornado path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the bigger tornado events for southern WI over the past 10-15 years with 2 EF-2s occurring and 8 tornadoes in total.  Juneau and Mayville took the brunt of the two strong tornadoes, while hundreds of large hail reports and several damaging wind reports were received across southern WI. A strong cold front, extending from strong low pressure over eastern North Dakota, combined with very unstable air and strong wind shear to bring widespread severe thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage path from this tornado which started southwest of Juneau, went through the north side of downtown Juneau, to the south of Horicon, hitting the Horicon Hills Golf Club, before ending east of Horicon along Highway 33.  The most intense damage was in the city of Juneau where about 10 structures hit EF-2 levels of damage.  The one minor injury from this tornado occurred in downtown Juneau where a man was sleeping in his bedroom when the roof blew off and a tree fell into the house.  The Clearview facility had windows blown out and one housing building lost it's roof.  Another residential building, which looked like a motel, lost a long section of its roof, but was poorly attached to the frame of the building. An empty silo was buckled on the east end of Home Rd with one cow having a two by four impaled into its back.  At the Horicon Hill Golf Club, 165 trees were either uprooted or damaged extensively.  Numerous golf carts were tipped or displaced and the main club house sustained extensive roof damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began on the north end of the Dodge County Airport, damaging 5 airport hangars and one airplane. The tornado continued east and went through the far south side of Horicon near River Bend Park, causing mainly tree damage along the rest of its path along with some minor barn and housing damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near Rib Falls at 5:41 pm CDT, then tracked north before lifting at 5:50 pm CDT. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near Bancroft at 5:52 pm CDT, then tracked north before lifting at 6:03 pm CDT. The start of the tornado was noted by trees across a road near the start of the path, with the rest of the tornado occurring over open area that was void of clear damage indicators. Total path length is estimated to be 5.21 miles long, with a width of 45 yards.  Peak wind estimated to be around 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of storms traveled northeast through western Wisconsin during the late afternoon of May 15th. There was one tornado in St Croix County and several locations of damaging wind including areas of significant wind damage in Dunn, Chippewa, Barron, and Rusk Counties. Very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter fell in Eau Claire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near a property along 110th Ave, uprooting multiple trees. The tornado tracked north toward County Road E, snapping tree trunks along the way. It then started to track more to the northeast, destroying a farm outbuilding along with multiple uprooted trees at a property along 160th Street.  The debris from this outbuilding was found along the rest of the tornado track. The most substantial damage occurred at a property along 165th Street, where multiple trees were snapped and a silo sustained significant damage. The silo was shifted, had one side caved in, and had its top removed. After this, the tornado tracked north of 140th Avenue before roping out and dissipating before reaching County Road G.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of storms traveled northeast through western Wisconsin during the late afternoon of May 15th. There was one tornado in St Croix County and several locations of damaging wind including areas of significant wind damage in Dunn, Chippewa, Barron, and Rusk Counties. Very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter fell in Eau Claire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey determined a downburst produced damage along a 4 mile long path, with a swath that broadened out to two miles wide. Winds were estimated at 75 to 85 mph. Many dozens of trees were toppled or broken. Part of a barn roof was blown off and another barn was shifted off its foundation. Two homes had some shingles blown off along with damaged fascia and gutters. A flagpole rated for 80 mph was snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey found many dozens of trees (possibly several hundred) were downed, mostly along the north side of Pine Lake and on the east side of Holcombe Flowage, northwest of the village of Holcombe. Some trees landed on sheds, houses, and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these storms as they moved across northwestern Wisconsin. Extensive tree damage was reported across the Bayfield Peninsula with hundreds of trees snapped about 4 to 8 feet above the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed in the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and nearby stationary front led to multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms across southern WI. Large hail and damaging straight line winds led to trees down and some structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe storms produced large and destructive hail over portions of southwestern Wisconsin during the pre-dawn hours on April 18th. Numerous instances of hail of golf ball sized or greater were reported across southern Grant County with the largest being baseball sized in Platteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail in Richland Center. The report was relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms moved through portions of west-central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours. The first round resulted in an isolated storm in Clark County that produced hail up to half dollar sized. A second round progressed through the area which produced a brief tornado northeast of Mondovi, Wisconsin and an isolated wind gust to 60 mph in Clark County.

The first round resulted in an isolated storm in Clark County that produced hail up to half dollar sized. A second round progressed through the area which produced a brief tornado northeast of Mondovi, Wisconsin and an isolated wind gust to 60 mph in Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management estimated 60 mph wind gusts about 4 miles west of Withee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms with very large hail and heavy rain tracked along a warm front over southern WI during the morning. Numerous reports of very large hail were received, and also flash flooding in Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of lanes were closed on several highways due to high water. This included I-43 from Hampton Ave. to State St., highway 175 from Lloyd St. to Wisconsin Ave., and I-41 from Good Hope Rd. to North Ave. There was fairly significant street flooding at 20th and Capitol Dr. and on Humboldt Blvd. in the Riverwest neighborhood on the east side.  Other street flooding occurred across all of northern Milwaukee County.

Strong to severe thunderstorms had developed across a surface cold front that moved east across western Wisconsin during the evening hours of April 28th. Increased southerly moisture transport from a +50kt low-level jet reached as far north as central Wisconsin. ||This line of storms also produced four tornadoes solely within Eau Claire County. An additional tornado developed in Buffalo County before crossing into Eau Claire County briefly before lifting. Maximum estimated wind speeds from the tornadoes varied from 80 to 95 mph. Damage included many uprooted and snapped trees, a pole barn being destroyed, damage to a farm outbuilding, and some roof/shingle damage to a few homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado took part of the roof off of a barn. It also damaged a barn door and broke several trees.  The maximum estimated wind speed was 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms had developed across a surface cold front that moved east across western Wisconsin during the evening hours of April 28th. An additional tornado developed in Buffalo County before crossing into Eau Claire County briefly before lifting. Maximum estimated wind speeds from the tornadoes varied from 80 to 95 mph. Damage included many uprooted and snapped trees, a pole barn being destroyed, damage to a farm outbuilding, and some roof/shingle damage to a few homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short lived tornado moved mostly along County Road M. It took part of the roof off of an outbuilding at a farm. Some shingles were also torn off the roofs of several houses. Several hundred trees were broken before it dissipated just east of County Road H.  The maximum estimated wind speed was 90 mph.

Showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning and into the afternoon ahead of an approaching warm front on Monday (4/28).  Storms during the morning and afternoon mainly produced hail up to quarter size. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening ahead of an approaching cold front on Monday (4/28).  The storms during the evening produced large hail up to quarter size, damaging winds, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell near Fremont.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water surrounds seasonal homes along Old River Rd. in the Blackhawk Park area including water at the base of Caledonia/Lewiston Levee on Old River Rd. Old River Rd, W. Lake Rd, Sec K Rd., Blackhawk Rd. are covered with water. West Lane Rd. in the Long Lake area has 4 feet of water over it in places. In the Town of Dekorra, about 12 homes are flooded on Hwy V. Water is over River Oaks Rd. and Oak Knoll Dr. near Hwy V.

The combination of rainfall and snow melt led to minor flooding along the Yellow River at Necedah beginning on April 2nd and continuing through the early morning hours on April 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yellow River at Necedah entered flood stage during the early morning on April 2nd before rising to a crest of 16.45 feet during the evening hours on the 2nd in minor flood stage. The site returned below flood stage during the early morning hours on April 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black River at Black River Falls entered minor flood stage after a period of heavy rain during the afternoon on April 21st and fell below flood stage during the morning hours the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Black River at Black River Falls went into minor flood stage on April 21st during the afternoon before cresting at a height of 49.15 feet. The river stage eventually fell below flood stage during the morning hours on April 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rainy period over the upper and middle WI River valley raised water levels to flood stage at the Wisconsin River at Portage for late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water surrounds seasonal homes along Old River Rd. in the Blackhawk Park area including water at the base of Caledonia/Lewiston Levee on Old River Rd. Old River Rd, W. Lake Rd, Sec K Rd., Blackhawk Rd. are covered with water. West Lane Rd. in the Long Lake area has 4 feet of water over it in places. In the Town of Dekorra, several homes on Hwy V have water on the 1st floor. Water is partially over River Oaks Rd. and Oak Knoll Dr. near Hwy V.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of rainfall, sometimes heavy, resulted in the flooding of a portion of the Root River Parkway in West Allis. A portion of the parkway was washed out and closed. The heavy rains were due to approaching low pressure and a warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Root River Parkway between National Ave. and 116th St. in West Allis was closed for several hours due to high water and a partial washout of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on June 25th a complex of storms produced three weak tornadoes across west-central Wisconsin that resulted primarily in tree damage. A second round of storms moved through the area on June 26th, producing one EF-0 tornado in Vernon County which caused minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed near State Highway 33 several miles south of Barre Mills and caused extensive tree damage in the Valleywood Springs area. This tornado then moved northeast causing additional spotty tree damage before dissipating near the intersection of County Highways M and I. Valleywood Springs would be impacted by a second tornado 10 minutes later with each tornado having its own circulation and debris signature on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region late Friday night (6/20) into early Saturday morning (6/21) allowing daytime temperatures to soar into the lower to middle 90s with dew points in the middle to upper 70s Saturday (6/21) and Sunday (6/22) afternoon.  The high of 94 at the Green Bay Airport on Sunday (6/22) tied the record high for the day. The combination of hot temperatures and high dew points allowed heat index values to soar to 105 to 110 degrees both days. Temperatures on Monday (6/23) again soared into the lower 90s with dew points in the lower 70s, pushing heat index values to 100 to 105 degrees.  Several highways buckled due to the extreme heat on Sunday (6/23).||In addition to the hot temperatures, overnight lows only fell into the upper 70s Saturday night (6/21) into early Sunday morning (6/22) as well as Sunday night (6/22) into early Monday morning (6/23), providing minimal relief from the hot temperatures. The warm overnight temperatures set or tied new records for record high minimums across much of the area for Sunday (6/22). The high minimum values on Sunday (6/22) of 79 in Green Bay, 78 in Wausau, and 76 in Rhinelander each tied the all-time highest minimum temperatures on record for those locations. The heat wave broke when a cold front moved through the area Monday afternoon and evening (6/23) along with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot temperatures and high humidity caused heat index values to rise as high as 107 degrees during the late afternoon on Saturday and 105 degrees Sunday afternoon.

The high of 94 at the Green Bay Airport on Sunday (6/22) tied the record high for the day. The combination of hot temperatures and high dew points allowed heat index values to soar to 105 to 110 degrees both days. Temperatures on Monday (6/23) again soared into the lower 90s with dew points in the lower 70s, pushing heat index values to 100 to 105 degrees.  Several highways buckled due to the extreme heat on Sunday (6/23).||In addition to the hot temperatures, overnight lows only fell into the upper 70s Saturday night (6/21) into early Sunday morning (6/22) as well as Sunday night (6/22) into early Monday morning (6/23), providing minimal relief from the hot temperatures. The warm overnight temperatures set or tied new records for record high minimums across much of the area for Sunday (6/22). The high minimum values on Sunday (6/22) of 79 in Green Bay, 78 in Wausau, and 76 in Rhinelander each tied the all-time highest minimum temperatures on record for those locations. The heat wave broke when a cold front moved through the area Monday afternoon and evening (6/23) along with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot temperatures and high humidity caused heat index values to rise as high as 106 degrees during the afternoon on Saturday and again on Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and gusty southwest winds due to strong low pressure over the Great Plains resulted in tree limbs and power lines down in Milwaukee. 15,280 customers lost power during the afternoon and was not fully restored until the next afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at General Mitchell International Airport measured a wind gust of 47 mph during the afternoon. Scattered tree limbs and powers lines down resulting in 15,280 customers without power for the afternoon and night during a heat wave. Power wasn't fully restored until the next day for some customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout the overnight hours and into the morning on February 8th, a winter storm moved through the region with a narrow but intense band of heavy snow across portions of north-central Wisconsin. Within this narrow axis of heavy snow, amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches with some locally higher amounts. Amounts quickly diminished along and south of Interstate 94 in Wisconsin with amounts around or under 2 inches observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 13 inches across Taylor County from this winter system. The highest total in the county of 13 inches was reported 2 miles east of Medford.

Amounts quickly diminished along and south of Interstate 94 in Wisconsin with amounts around or under 2 inches observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged between 2 to 10 inches across Clark County from this winter system. The highest total in the county of 9.8 inches was in Withee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southwest winds pumped hot and humid air into southern WI for a 3 day period. Heat index values rose into the 100s during the day and only dropped into the 70s at night. Cooling centers were opened in urban areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon on Sunday (6/08).  The strongest storms produced quarter size hail across central Wisconsin. Lightning struck an apartment building in De Pere during the early evening, causing electrical damage and a hole in the roof where lightning hit the building. The lightning produced around $40,000 in damage to the apartment building and displaced 24 residents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an apartment building in De Pere causing electrical damage and a hole in the roof where the lightning hit the building. The lightning produced around $40,000 in damage and displaced 24 residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked through central and east-central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening on Wednesday (7/16) as a mesoscale convective vortex tracked along a cold front through southern Wisconsin.  This system brought a brief period of heavy rainfall with rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches and locally higher amounts exceeding 5 inches across the area.  The heavy rain in a short period caused flash flooding as streets were covered in flowing water with multiple streets closed and vehicles stranded.||Some of the highest rainfall totals included 5.57 inches in Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.), 5.52 inches in Poy Sippi (Waushara Co.), 4.95 inches in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.), 4.75 inches in Wautoma (Waushara Co.), and 3.95 inches in Menasha (Winnebago Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees in Richford. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeasterly, onshore winds and waves of 3-7 ft occurred along Lake Michigan beaches across southeastern WI resulting in conditions for dangerous currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Racine Sheriff's Office said the 15-year-old went under while swimming at Carre-Hogle Park (2 S Racine in Racine County) and did not resurface. She was later pulled from the water approximately 20 feet from shore. Reported conditions were very poor, with 20���25 mph sustained winds, 3 feet waves in the lagoon area (behind a break wall) and visible rip currents observed by first responders. Also noted conditions were making rescue difficult. Buoy 45199 (Salmon Unlimited) reported 5-6 ft waves around the time a few miles offshore from event. MKX had a Beach Hazard Statement in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these storms as they moved across northwestern Wisconsin. Extensive tree damage was reported across the Bayfield Peninsula with hundreds of trees snapped about 4 to 8 feet above the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large poplar and one partially dead pine tree were snapped part way up the trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 3 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations up to 0.1 inches of flat ice across the county. A narrow band of heavy snow resulted in accumulations around 1 to 3 inches on top of the ice, leading to hazardous travel conditions Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, March 4 to Wednesday, March 5, 2025, a powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and strong wind across the Upper Midwest. As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 13 inches fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassable, leading to dozens of school closures in west central Wisconsin. There were also power outages.||The highest reported snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches southwest of Ellsworth, WI (Pierce).|11.0 inches in Woodville, WI (St Croix).|11.0 inches in East Farmington, WI (Polk).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 4 to 8 inches of snowfall was measured across the county with the greatest amounts across the southeast portion.

As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 13 inches fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassable, leading to dozens of school closures in west central Wisconsin. There were also power outages.||The highest reported snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches southwest of Ellsworth, WI (Pierce).|11.0 inches in Woodville, WI (St Croix).|11.0 inches in East Farmington, WI (Polk).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 6 to 10 inches of snowfall was measured across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 3 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations around 0.1 to 0.2 inches of flat ice across the county. A narrow band of heavy snow resulted in accumulations around 1 to 3 inches on top of the ice, leading to hazardous travel conditions Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Damaging winds were the primary threat with these storms as they moved across northwestern Wisconsin. Extensive tree damage was reported across the Bayfield Peninsula with hundreds of trees snapped about 4 to 8 feet above the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several branches were broken off due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked through central and east-central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening on Wednesday (7/16) as a mesoscale convective vortex tracked along a cold front through southern Wisconsin.  This system brought a brief period of heavy rainfall with rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches and locally higher amounts exceeding 5 inches across the area.  The heavy rain in a short period caused flash flooding as streets were covered in flowing water with multiple streets closed and vehicles stranded.||Some of the highest rainfall totals included 5.57 inches in Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.), 5.52 inches in Poy Sippi (Waushara Co.), 4.95 inches in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.), 4.75 inches in Wautoma (Waushara Co.), and 3.95 inches in Menasha (Winnebago Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in a short time caused flash flooding in the city of Oshkosh and just south of Neenah. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief tornado crossed Interstate 90 and was captured on video. Some corn and small tree branches were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across northwestern Wisconsin the morning of the 4th bringing a very warm and humid airmass to the region. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours with a few being severe across Burnett and Washburn counties. Sporadic wind damage to trees was reported along with some marginally severe hail. Areas just west of the town of Shell Lake in Bashaw Township in Washburn County saw extensive damage due to flash flooding caused by slow moving storms with very heavy rainfall. Numerous roads and culverts in the area were completely washed out and repairs were not completely for 2 to 3 weeks in some cases due to the extent of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported the power company had 15 power poles down along Crosstown Road east-southeast of Grantsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms tracked across southern Wisconsin from early afternoon through the early evening hours.  Numerous supercell thunderstorms developed, producing a number of wall and funnel clouds as well as a few tornadoes.  A secondary line of storms came through which caused sporadic wind damage.  The main damage was in Iowa county from the strongest tornado which occurred near and northeast of Dodgeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado started along Highway 78 near Racek Road and went through the hills near the Black Hawk Unit before crossing County Highway Y, ending Just across Highway 12.  The main area of damage was to the Birchwood Drive neighborhood where about 20 to 40 trees were uprooted or had multiple limbs broken off.  Multiple storm chaser videos show the main tornado tracking just along and to the west of this neighborhood. The only structural damage noted that wasn't from falling trees was to one house on Birchwood Drive that had a few shingles pulled up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours on July 28th and into the very early morning hours on the 29th, a line of weakening thunderstorms crossed the Mississippi River into Wisconsin. Initially, these storms produced widespread wind damage as they crossed the river with multiple gusts to around 60 to 70 mph. However, as they pushed further east into Wisconsin they quickly weakened below severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a 66 mph wind gust 4 miles southwest of Mondovi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms and a few supercells developed ahead of a warm front Wednesday (7/23) afternoon across central and north-central Wisconsin. The storms eventually developed into a Quasi-Linear Convective System and tracked through the rest of central, east-central, and northeast Wisconsin through the early evening.  The strongest storms produced damaging winds and 5 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Gillett at 5:37 PM CDT.  The tornado tracked east and tore the roof of a metal building, uplifted and damaged the roof of a nearby building, and snapped and uprooted several trees before dissipating east of Gillett at 5:40 PM CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 2.33 miles long, with a width of 65 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms and a few supercells developed ahead of a warm front Wednesday (7/23) afternoon across central and north-central Wisconsin. The storms eventually developed into a Quasi-Linear Convective System and tracked through the rest of central, east-central, and northeast Wisconsin through the early evening.  The strongest storms produced damaging winds and 5 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down west of Oconto at 6:00 PM CDT.  The tornado tracked northeast and snapped and uprooted several trees before dissipating just north of Oconto at 6:04 PM CDT. The tornado uprooted several trees at a local golf course northwest of Oconto. Total path length is estimated to be 2.37 miles long, with a width of 65 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms tracked across southern Wisconsin from early afternoon through the early evening hours.  Numerous supercell thunderstorms developed, producing a number of wall and funnel clouds as well as a few tornadoes.  A secondary line of storms came through which caused sporadic wind damage.  The main damage was in Iowa county from the strongest tornado which occurred near and northeast of Dodgeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding in Fond du Lac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours on July 28th and into the very early morning hours on the 29th, a line of weakening thunderstorms crossed the Mississippi River into Wisconsin. Initially, these storms produced widespread wind damage as they crossed the river with multiple gusts to around 60 to 70 mph. However, as they pushed further east into Wisconsin they quickly weakened below severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a large maple tree downed in the city of La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked through central and east-central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening on Wednesday (7/16) as a mesoscale convective vortex tracked along a cold front through southern Wisconsin.  This system brought a brief period of heavy rainfall with rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches and locally higher amounts exceeding 5 inches across the area.  The heavy rain in a short period caused flash flooding as streets were covered in flowing water with multiple streets closed and vehicles stranded.||Some of the highest rainfall totals included 5.57 inches in Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.), 5.52 inches in Poy Sippi (Waushara Co.), 4.95 inches in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.), 4.75 inches in Wautoma (Waushara Co.), and 3.95 inches in Menasha (Winnebago Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4 to 5.5 inches in a short time caused flash flooding on highway 21 between Coloma and Wautoma as well as in the city of Wautoma. Highway 21 was closed between Coloma and Wautoma with multiple roads closed in Wautoma due to street flooding. Multiple vehicles were stalled with passengers rescued by boat. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms tracked through east-central Wisconsin during the afternoon hours on Saturday (7/19).  The storms spun up a brief tornado in Manitowoc County as a thunderstorm interacted with the lake breeze boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Branch at 4:21 PM CDT.  The tornado tracked southeast and snapped and uprooted some trees and produced siding and roofing damage near the start of the tornado, tracking over farm fields with damage evident on satellite data before dissipating just northeast of Branch at 4:25 PM CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 1.58 miles long, with a width of 60 yards.  Peak wind estimated to be around 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was draped across western Wisconsin during the day on the 15th. This led to multiple rounds of storms across northwestern Wisconsin during the morning and afternoon hours. Some of the storms contained damaging winds that led to tree damage across parts of Washburn, Sawyer, Ashland, and Bayfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along County Highway F between County Highway K and Highway 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over western MN and progressed east-southeast during the evening of July 28th, 2025. As this line of storms reached western WI, the intensity remained consistent across the region, producing 60 to 70 mph damaging wind gusts across an area extending from St. Croix to Pepin Counties, then eastward to Chippewa and Eau Claire Counties. ||There were numerous reports of trees down and power outages across several areas. Although measured gusts in Pierce Co and Chippewa Counties were between 60 to 65 mph, the degree of damage that occurred in New Richmond (St. Croix) indicated an isolated swath of wind gusts just over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down across road 2 miles northwest of Durand or 1.5 miles north of the intersection of Co Rd D and WI-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary dropped across the Northland the 2nd into the 3rd. A few thunderstorms formed ahead of the front during the afternoon hours of the 2nd, but were mainly non-severe with limited instability in place. A few storms were a bit stronger and produced isolated wind damage in parts of Sawyer and Washburn counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped along Sawmill Road on the northeast side of Hayward Lake. At least one of the trees was larger than two feet in diameter per photos. One of the trees fell onto a home causing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across northwestern Wisconsin the morning of the 4th bringing a very warm and humid airmass to the region. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours with a few being severe across Burnett and Washburn counties. Sporadic wind damage to trees was reported along with some marginally severe hail. Areas just west of the town of Shell Lake in Bashaw Township in Washburn County saw extensive damage due to flash flooding caused by slow moving storms with very heavy rainfall. Numerous roads and culverts in the area were completely washed out and repairs were not completely for 2 to 3 weeks in some cases due to the extent of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of showers and thunderstorms produced hail across portions of central Wisconsin during the morning on Saturday (8/09).  ||A stronger line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and tracked through central, east-central Wisconsin and the lakeshore ahead of an approaching cold front. These storms produced widespread wind damage with Door County included in a disaster declaration due to the significant storm damage.  The Door County Emergency Manager indicated there was $431,938 worth of damage and debris clean up across central and northern Door County.  Egg Harbor and Ephraim experienced significant damage, with Sister Bay and Baileys Harbor notably affected. ||The storms also produced a weak, brief tornado in Peninsula State Park (Door Co.) with lightning causing a house fire and significant damage to a home in Casco (Kewaunee Co.), producing around $125,000 worth of damage. In total, this event produced over a half a million worth of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Green Bay. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flash flood and record rainfall occurred over portions of the Milwaukee Metropolitan Area and Southeast WI resulting in damages in the $100s Millions. Warm, moist, and unstable air rose along a stalled front to produce numerous thunderstorms and more than 10 inches of rain in many locations. Widespread flash flooding of streets, creeks, rivers, and low lying neighborhoods occurred. A homeless camp in Milwaukee underneath 1st Street Bridge adjacent to the surge of record flooding on the Kinnickinnic River was swept away. One or two deaths may be a result of this flash flooding with a couple others missing. Thousands of residential homes or businesses sustained major flood damage with dozens classified as destroyed due to 1st floor flooding, basement flooding or foundation collapse. Residential sewage backups, and raw sewage released into rivers and Lake MI by many municipalities was common.   A small number of complete road washouts and swift water rescues occurred, in addition to dozens of people stranded in their vehicles in deep, still water in low lying areas. First responders had difficulty getting to their 911 calls including stranded motorists and flooded neighborhoods, due to the numerous flooded roads. Dozens of people were evacuated or relocated from their flooded homes and neighborhoods. Longer duration areal flooding and river flooding, which ranged from moderate flooding to record flooding, then continued for a couple more days. In addition to the flash flooding, a couple hikers were struck by lightning, via a ground current from a nearby strike on a tree, on the Ice Age Trail near Palmyra. They were initially rendered unconscious but did survive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuation of widespread flash flooding impacts occurring countywide. Delayed flash flooding onset across far northern portions of Milwaukee County, where heavy rain arrived later in the overnight hours of August 9-10, 2025. Additional numerous reports of flash flooding near rivers, creeks, and streams. Numerous reports of water in basements across the impacted area. Portion of 47th street between Churchill Ln & Willow Rd completely washed away from flooding of an unnamed creek in Brown Deer. Flooding impacts contribute to an estimated $65,813,855 in public and individual infrastructure damage across Milwaukee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate rounds of storms moved through Grant County on August 10th bringing both heavy rain and flash flooding along with them. The first round of storms moved through during the early morning hours bringing widespread flooding issues to northern portions of the county. The worst of the flash flooding was in the town of Bagley where a debris blockage resulted in storm water inundating residences in the town. Later in the afternoon, a secondary round of storms moved through the area which brought additional instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over highway 133 in Blue River with some homes being evacuated. Additionally, street flooding was reported by emergency management in Boscobel in northern Grant County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread and numerous reports of flash flooding in streets, in addition to rivers, creeks, and streams across the southern two thirds of Milwaukee County. 1800 homes were classified as major damage or destroyed due to flash flooding of the Lincoln and Honey Creeks. Many instances of flooded basements across the impacted area. Six foundations collapsed into basements of homes in the 60th street neighborhood near Lincoln Creek, with two homes being condemned. Additional basement collapse reported in West Allis near Honey Creek. Many vehicles stranded in flood waters, with multiple water rescues conducted by the Milwaukee Fire Department. Water reached the bottom of car doors at Wisconsin State Fair Park. Reports of water inside vehicles along Delaware Avenue in Bay View. Multiple runway and taxiway closures reported at Mitchell International Airport and Timmerman Field from flooding. Impacts contribute to an estimated $65,813,855 in public and individual infrastructure damages countywide.  Brief (30-45 minute total duration) rises into moderate flood stage along the Kinnickinnic River at Milwaukee and Oak Creek in South Milwaukee between 12:30 & 2:30 AM local time on August 10. At 10 feet, water impacts the yards of homes along Oak Creek in South Milwaukee. At 16 feet, flood water is approximately 1 foot over the top of the concrete-lined channel of the Kinnickinnic River at Milwaukee, affecting homes in the residential neighborhood next to the river. Homeless camp swept away as a result of rapid rises along the Kinnickinnic River, leaving some dead and missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of strong to severe storms throughout the day on August 16th resulted in instances of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding in western Wisconsin. Hail with these storms reached up to golf ball sized near New Lisbon along with instances of tree damage. Later into the evening, additional storms moved through Grant County and produced heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding in Lancaster along with some quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down on power line on CTY H north of Hwy 21 about 5 miles northwest of Cutler. Nearby wind observation in Cutler gusted to 44 mph.

They were initially rendered unconscious but did survive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding of streets, creeks, rivers, and low lying neighborhoods and homes occurred especially in New Berlin, Brookfield, Menomonee Falls, Waukesha, Pewaukee, Merton, and Muskego. Some of the more notable creeks or rivers that flooded were the Fox River, Jewel Creek, and their tributaries, as well as some lake flooding.  Some flood damage occurred to homes especially near Linnie Lac Lake toward Little Muskego Lake. These damages were from a variety of 1st floor flooding, basement flooding, and foundation collapse. Residential sewage backups, and raw sewage released into rivers by many municipalities were common.  A small number of complete road washouts and swift water rescues occurred, in addition to dozens of people stranded in their vehicles in deep, still water in low lying areas. More specifically, one lane of Gold Dr. washed out due to the flash flooding of Jewel Creek in Muskego. Large sections of Barker Road in Brookfield and other nearby roads were closed due to the flash flooding of the Fox River, including the Fox River overtopping the Barker Rd. bridge. Dozens of people were evacuated or relocated from their flooded homes and neighborhoods, which included the Deer Path Subdivision near Minooka Park. A sidewall of the Merton Millpond Dam was damaged but quickly repaired. Many other dams in eastern Waukesha County were at or exceeding their maximum flow capacity, but no dam failures occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lingering areal flooding impacts following the conclusion of flash flooding impacts. Lingering road closures from standing water and rivers/creeks that remain out of their banks. Lingering reports of water in basements, in addition to inundation of properties from standing water. Record crest of 11.25 feet (major flood stage) occurs along the Milwaukee River at Estabrook Park at 4:15 AM local time August 10, with the river remaining in major flood stage until 9 AM local time August 10 and moderate flood stage until 11:15 AM local time August 10. In moderate flood stage, waters from the Milwaukee River at Estabrook Park lead to widespread flooding in the Glendale area, with floodwaters inundating much of Lincoln and Kletsch Parks. Numerous Glendale streets are affected by floodwaters, including but not limited to Bender Rd, West Riverview Dr, North Forest Dr, and North Sunny Point Rd. Moderate river flooding occurs at the Root River in Franklin, with a record crest of 11.71 feet occurring at 3:30 PM local time August 10, and the river remaining in moderate flood stage until 7:15 AM local time August 11. In moderate flood stage, floodwaters from the Root River at Franklin inundate the Root River Pkwy, 8 Mile Rd, 27th St, in addition to yards along the river. Floodwaters from the Root River lead to widespread flooding of agricultural land and lowlands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pepin County Sheriff's Office reported that a portion of County Highway N was closed due to heavy rain and flooding just north of Ella.

Other roads flooded and closed due to the flooding of the Milwaukee River were a portion of Green Bay Rd. in Thiensville, River Rd. from Donges Bay Rd. to County Line Rd., and Island Dr. from East Shoreland Dr. to Circle Dr. Sections of I-43 were closed due to flooding. This included I-43 northbound from Pioneer Rd. to Hwy 60. Only one lane of I-43 southbound was open. The I-43 southbound exit ramps to Grafton and Mequon Rd. were closed. Moderate flooding of the Cedar Creek at Cedarburg occurs during the morning and afternoon hours of August 12, with a crest of 11.03 feet occurring at 10:15 AM local time August 12. Moderate river flooding of the Cedar Creek at Cedarburg leads to the closure of Cedar Creek Park in downtown Cedarburg, in addition to the inundation of backyards of some homes and businesses along Sheboygan Rd in the community of Cedarburg. Moderate river flooding continues until 3 PM local time August 12.
