EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding of streets, creeks, rivers, and low lying neighborhoods and homes continued over the eastern half of the county and was aggravated by additional showers and storms.  The greatest concentration of flooding was in New Berlin, Brookfield, Butler, Menomonee Falls, Waukesha, Pewaukee, Merton, and Muskego. Some of the more notable creeks or rivers that flooded were the Fox River, Menomonee River, Jewel Creek, Butler Ditch, Root River, and their tributaries, as well as some lake flooding. Some flood damage occurred to homes especially near Linnie Lac toward Little Muskego Lake. These damages were from a variety of 1st floor flooding, basement flooding, and foundation collapse. Residential sewage backups, and raw sewage released into rivers by many municipalities were common. A small number of complete road washouts and swift water rescues occurred, in addition to dozens of people stranded in their vehicles in deep, still water in low lying areas. More specifically, one lane of Gold Dr. washed out due to the flash flooding of Jewel Creek in Muskego. Large sections of Barker Road in Brookfield and other nearby roads were closed due to the flash flooding of the Fox River, including the Fox River overtopping the Barker Rd. bridge. A partial road washout occurred on Lily Road in Menomonee Falls due to the flash flooding of a tributary of the Menomonee River. The large collapse of a Y-shaped intersection at Campbell Dr. and Campbell Ct. in Menomonee Falls also occurred due to the flash flooding of Butler Ditch. 130 St. in New Berlin was partially washed out from the flash flooding of a tributary of the Root River. Dozens of people were evacuated or relocated from their flooded homes and neighborhoods, which included the Deer Path Subdivision near Minooka Park and homes near Linnie Lac. A sidewall of the Merton Millpond Dam was damaged but quickly repaired. Many other dams in eastern Waukesha County were at or exceeding their maximum flow capacity, but no dam failures occurred. River flooding along the Lower Fox River in Waukesha peaks during the evening of August 11, with a 8.62 foot crest in moderate flood stage occurring at 5:15 PM local time. Moderate river flooding continues along Lower Fox River in Waukesha through the morning hours of August 13. In moderate flood stage, water from the Lower Fox River approaches the base of the Bastow St and Bank St Bridges in downtown Waukesha, in addition to some homes in the Bethesda Court area about one half mile south of downtown Waukesha. Water also inundates the Frame Park area, with waters at least 3 feet deep on land near the shoreline of the Lower Fox River. Water approaches Corrina Blvd and Union St to the south of downtown Waukesha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved out of southeast Minnesota and northeast Iowa and rapidly developed a strong wind signature on radar as they progressed through Vernon County. While this signature was relatively short-lived, severe winds resulted in instances of trees down in both Vernon and Monroe counties prior to the storms weakening and moving eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down near Westby.

Later into the evening, additional storms moved through Grant County and produced heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding in Lancaster along with some quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of showers and thunderstorms produced hail across portions of central Wisconsin during the morning on Saturday (8/09).  ||A stronger line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and tracked through central, east-central Wisconsin and the lakeshore ahead of an approaching cold front. These storms produced widespread wind damage with Door County included in a disaster declaration due to the significant storm damage.  The Door County Emergency Manager indicated there was $431,938 worth of damage and debris clean up across central and northern Door County.  Egg Harbor and Ephraim experienced significant damage, with Sister Bay and Baileys Harbor notably affected. ||The storms also produced a weak, brief tornado in Peninsula State Park (Door Co.) with lightning causing a house fire and significant damage to a home in Casco (Kewaunee Co.), producing around $125,000 worth of damage. In total, this event produced over a half a million worth of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down near Ephraim in Peninsula State Park at 3:54 PM CDT.  The most significant damage was an uprooted tree with the NWS on the low end of the damage scale due to saturated ground.  The tornado crossed into Eagle Harbor as a waterspout at 3:55 pm CDT. Total path length is estimated to be 0.25 miles long, with a width of 32 yards. Peak wind estimated to be around 73 mph.

Warm, moist, and unstable air rose along a stalled front to produce numerous thunderstorms and more than 10 inches of rain in many locations. Widespread flash flooding of streets, creeks, rivers, and low lying neighborhoods occurred. A homeless camp in Milwaukee underneath 1st Street Bridge adjacent to the surge of record flooding on the Kinnickinnic River was swept away. One or two deaths may be a result of this flash flooding with a couple others missing. Thousands of residential homes or businesses sustained major flood damage with dozens classified as destroyed due to 1st floor flooding, basement flooding or foundation collapse. Residential sewage backups, and raw sewage released into rivers and Lake MI by many municipalities was common.   A small number of complete road washouts and swift water rescues occurred, in addition to dozens of people stranded in their vehicles in deep, still water in low lying areas. First responders had difficulty getting to their 911 calls including stranded motorists and flooded neighborhoods, due to the numerous flooded roads. Dozens of people were evacuated or relocated from their flooded homes and neighborhoods. Longer duration areal flooding and river flooding, which ranged from moderate flooding to record flooding, then continued for a couple more days. In addition to the flash flooding, a couple hikers were struck by lightning, via a ground current from a nearby strike on a tree, on the Ice Age Trail near Palmyra. They were initially rendered unconscious but did survive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The continued areal flooding of streets, creeks, rivers, and low lying neighborhoods in northern Cedarburg, Grafton, Port Washington and other areas of central and northern Ozaukee County. Sections of I-43 were closed due to flooding. This included I-43 northbound from Pioneer Rd. to Hwy 60. Only one lane of I-43 southbound was open. The I-43 southbound exit ramp to Grafton was closed. The Milwaukee River flooded and closed Riverside Dr. north of highway 33 in Saukville as well as other roads near the river. Some basement flooding continued during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of showers and thunderstorms produced hail across portions of central Wisconsin during the morning on Saturday (8/09).  ||A stronger line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and tracked through central, east-central Wisconsin and the lakeshore ahead of an approaching cold front. These storms produced widespread wind damage with Door County included in a disaster declaration due to the significant storm damage.  The Door County Emergency Manager indicated there was $431,938 worth of damage and debris clean up across central and northern Door County.  Egg Harbor and Ephraim experienced significant damage, with Sister Bay and Baileys Harbor notably affected. ||The storms also produced a weak, brief tornado in Peninsula State Park (Door Co.) with lightning causing a house fire and significant damage to a home in Casco (Kewaunee Co.), producing around $125,000 worth of damage. In total, this event produced over a half a million worth of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds collapsed a standing barn. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 3-vehicle accident occurred during the morning hours on October 30th, resulting in 1 fatality on highway 53 in northern La Crosse County. At the time of the accident, dense valley fog along the Mississippi River was producing visibilities of less than 1/4 mile nearby at the La Crosse Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3-vehicle accident resulted in 1 fatality during the morning hours along highway 53 north of Holmen in northern La Crosse County on October 30th. At the time of the incident, dense river valley fog was ongoing in the Mississippi River Valley with visibility reduced to less than 1/4 mile at the La Crosse Regional Airport nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were experienced over parts of Iron County for around 6 hours on the 26th. In addition to the blizzard conditions, very heavy snowfall occurred between the main system and the lake-effect following. Snowfall totals of 10 to 20 inches were common with a high report of 32.3 inches near Mercer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake-effect snowfall begin during the afternoon of the 8th and continued into the 10th when high pressure arrived and brought drier air and less favorable winds into the region, ending the event. Iron County received the most snowfall in this event with 9 to 12 inches of accumulation across the Penokee Range and 4 to 8 inches elsewhere. Ashland and Price counties saw lesser amounts with parts of those counties not receiving any snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall of 3 to 6 inches were reported across the county. The heaviest snowfall was in the far eastern and southern parts of the county. A high report of 7 inches was recorded near Butternut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking from northern Illinois to the central Great Lakes brought a swath of heavy snow to portions of central and east-central Wisconsin from Saturday (11/29) through Sunday morning (11/30). Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 10 inches across central to east-central Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10 inches in Kiel (Manitowoc Co.), 9 inches in Millhome (Manitowoc Co.), 8 inches in Mischicot (Manitowoc Co.) as well as in Waupaca (Waupaca Co.) and Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), 7.5 inches in Greenville (Outagamie Co.) as well as in Omro (Winnebago Co.), and 7 inches in Coloma (Waushara Co.) as well as in Larsen (Winnebago Co.), Algoma (Kewaunee Co.), and Appleton (Outagamie Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Calumet County included 7 inches in Chilton, 6.5 inches in New Holstein, and 6 inches in Brillion as well as near Combined Locks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced a late fall system that brought a round of rain and heavy snow, as well as very gusty winds to the Upper Midwest. The combination of heavy falling snow and northwest winds gusting to around 25 to 35 mph resulted in hazardous travel, especially across portions of northwest Wisconsin where the strongest winds overlapped with falling snow.||Snowfall reports were limited across the region due to the blowing and drifting that occurred overnight, so nearby reports were used to supplement the analysis. This include a 7.0 inch report near Shell Lake, WI and 7.4 inch report near Sarona near the Washburn/Barron county border, as well as a 6.5 inch report near Rush City in northeastern Chisago County.||The greatest snowfall amounts were reported along and just north of the northern portions of Polk, Barron, and Rusk counties, topping out around 4 to 7 inches. ||The top snowfall reports were:|4.8 inches in Turtle Lake, WI (Polk).|4.0 inches near Osceola, WI (Polk).|4.0 inches in Roberts, WI (St Croix).|3.7 inches in Woodville, WI (St Croix).|3.0 inches near Balsam Lake, WI (Polk).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 2 to 7 inches were estimated across the county, with the greatest amounts to the north. Winds also increased late Tuesday night, with gusts up to 30 mph measured at Osceola Municipal Airport into Wednesday morning.

Lake enhanced snow contributed to the snow totals from the Kettle Moraine ridge and eastward, but milder temperatures right at the lakeshore kept the snow totals down a bit in said area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 9-12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region just after midnight on November 29thm bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 13 inches across portions of western Wisconsin, primarily near and south of Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter storm brought between 7 and 9 inches of snowfall to Juneau County over the course of November 29th and 30th. The highest snowfall report in the county was near Union Center where 9 inches was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first signs of drought were noted in the late summer and early fall, August to September timeframe, when there were sporadic areas reporting below normal rainfall across northern Wisconsin. The dry conditions expanded and precipitation deficits increased across much of the area in September and continued into October. The peak in the dry spell occurred from November 1-23 when only a few tenths of an inch were recorded. A good majority of the precipitation for the month fell from the 24th through the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Rhinelander, below normal precipitation was recorded from September through November. The October precipitation total was 1.18 inches or 30% of normal. The peak in the dry spell occurred from November 1-23, with only 0.15 inches recorded, which was only 12% of the monthly total. The Drought Monitor on the 18th indicated that the southeast quarter of the county was designated in Severe Drought (D2), with Moderate Drought (D1) over the remainder of the county. The precipitation at the end of the month brought the monthly total to only 41% of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were reported across Washburn County for the event. A high report of 10.1 inches was recorded near Sarona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake-effect snow band developed off the Twin Ports over western Lake Superior and moved on-shore during the afternoon and evening hours of the 29th. The band then stalled out over parts of Superior dropping 4 to 7 inches of snow before finally moving to the east into Bayfield County. An area of Bayfield County just to the south of Cornucopia saw additional enhancement with accumulations of 5 to 9 inches. Additionally, synoptic snowfall occurred over a portion of the region as well with snowfall barely reaching warning levels in Burnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of lake effect snow set up over Superior the afternoon and evening hours of the 29th. This band was expected to quickly shift to the east, but ended up stalling out over the same area for an extended period of time. This led to snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches in town with a high report of 7 inches in the South End neighborhood of Superior.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect moved across the Bayfield Peninsula during the evening and early morning hours of the 29th into the 30th. This band was fairly transient by this point, but was still able to produce some very heavy snowfall in the Cornucopia area. Snowfall totals of 5 to 9 inches were reported in and near Cornucopia with a high report of 9.5 inches about six miles south of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced a late fall system that brought a round of rain and heavy snow, as well as very gusty winds to the Upper Midwest. The combination of heavy falling snow and northwest winds gusting to around 25 to 35 mph resulted in hazardous travel, especially across portions of northwest Wisconsin where the strongest winds overlapped with falling snow.||Snowfall reports were limited across the region due to the blowing and drifting that occurred overnight, so nearby reports were used to supplement the analysis. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 1 to 7 inches were estimated across the county, with the greatest amounts to the northwest and lower amounts to the southeast. Winds also increased Wednesday morning, with gusts up to 35 mph measured at Rusk County Regional Airport into Wednesday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking from the southern Great Plains into lower MI brought a long duration moderate to heavy snowfall to southern WI. 6-10 inches accumulated toward central WI with 8-14 inches over the remainder of southern WI, with the highest totals over far southern WI. Lake enhanced snow contributed to the snow totals from the Kettle Moraine ridge and eastward, but milder temperatures right at the lakeshore kept the snow totals down a bit in said area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 5-7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region just after midnight on November 29thm bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 13 inches across portions of western Wisconsin, primarily near and south of Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter storm brought between 9 and 13 inches of snowfall to Grant County over the course of November 29th and 30th. The highest snowfall report in the county was in Lancaster where 13 inches was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system tracked through Wisconsin Tuesday evening (11/25) and slowly tracked through the Upper Peninsula of Michigan on Wednesday (11/26). Rain changed over to snow Tuesday night (11/25) into Wednesday morning (11/26) across the region as west to northwest winds increased to 40 to 50 mph on Wednesday (11/26).  The combination of heavy snow and gusty winds caused significant blowing snow along with power outages across north-central Wisconsin. Over 9,000 were reported without power in Vilas County and over 2,000 in Oneida County.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 18 inches in Presque Isle (Vilas Co.), 12 inches in Rest Lake (Vilas Co.), 9 inches in Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.), and 7.2 inches in Minocqua (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 18 inches in Presque Isle, 12 inches in Rest Lake, 9 inches in Boulder Junction, and 8.5 inches near Boulder Junction. Over 9,000 were reported without power in Vilas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant lake effect snowstorm occurred for Racine and Kenosha when a polar air mass flowed over the relatively warm waters of Lake MI, producing 2-3 inch per hour snowfall rates. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 7-10.5 inches with snowfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across Price County were in the 5 to 8 inch range for the event. A high report of 10.6 inches was reported about 6 miles northwest of Butternut.

Most areas received 8 to 12 inches of accumulation with a high report of 17.5 inches near Brule. Near blizzard conditions were also reported at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking from northern WI into the northern Great Lakes brought a period of light snow accumulation combined with 40-50 mph wind gusts to create hazardous travel north of Milwaukee. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred. Accidents led to the closure of I-43 for some time in Ozaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one half inch or less of snow accumulation and wind gusts of 40-50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region just after midnight on November 29thm bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 13 inches across portions of western Wisconsin, primarily near and south of Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter storm brought between 5 and 7 inches of snowfall to Jackson County over the course of November 29th and 30th. The highest snowfall report in the county was around 4 miles west of Melrose where 6.8 inches was observed. Additionally, a vehicle accident on Interstate 94 near Black River Falls resulted in 1 fatality and 1 injury during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November saw the return of D2 Severe Drought conditions across parts of northwestern Wisconsin following an extended period of below normal precipitation extending back to the late summer. Starting with the update on the 4th, D2 Severe Drought was introduced across portions of Douglas, Burnett and Washburn counties. With the update on the 18th, D2 Severe Drought conditions expanded further in these areas. For the month, precipitation was around a quarter to three-quarters of an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions were introduced across the far western reaches and much of the southern part of Douglas County with the update on the 4th. With the update on the 18th, D2 Severe Drought further expanded covering much of the western 3/4ths of the county. No additional changes in drought category were observed over the remainder of the month. Precipitation for the month was around 0.25 to 0.75 inches below normal in this area with the dry pattern extending back into the summer months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking from the southern Great Plains into lower MI brought a long duration moderate to heavy snowfall to southern WI. 6-10 inches accumulated toward central WI with 8-14 inches over the remainder of southern WI, with the highest totals over far southern WI. Lake enhanced snow contributed to the snow totals from the Kettle Moraine ridge and eastward, but milder temperatures right at the lakeshore kept the snow totals down a bit in said area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 9-12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region just after midnight on November 29thm bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 13 inches across portions of western Wisconsin, primarily near and south of Interstate 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter storm brought around 5 and 7 inches of snowfall to Buffalo County over the course of November 29th and 30th. The highest snowfall report in the county was around 5 miles northeast of Montana where 7.4 inches was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spark from an ATV caused a fire to ignite in the marsh area near Redgranite on Sunday (11/16) around 11:30 am CST in eastern Waushara County.  Very dry conditions leading up to the fire along with gusty northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph and low relative humidity values in the middle 20s that day allowed the fire to spread to 436 acres. The fire led to the closure of parts of state Highways 21 and 49 and was visible on satellite as well as radar imagery. The fire was considered contained by local authorities by 5 pm CST. There were no injuries or fatalities reported from the fire with only one outbuilding impacted from the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spark from an ATV caused a fire to ignite in the marsh area near Redgranite on Sunday (11/16) around 11:30 am CST in eastern Waushara County. The fire led to the closure of parts of state Highways 21 and 49 and was visible on satellite as well as radar imagery. The fire was considered contained by local authorities by 5 pm CST. There were no injuries or fatalities reported from the fire with only one outbuilding impacted from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated cell developed along an instability gradient in the late afternoon of June 19th across eastern Minnesota. As the cell moved southeastward near Osceola, WI, the cell rapidly developed into a long-tracked supercell which produced a swath of quarter to golf ball size hail across St. Croix and Pierce Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter northwest of the New Richmond Regional Airport along County Highway CC and County Road C.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on September 22nd, individual storm cells developed along a frontal boundary and pushed south through western Wisconsin. As these storms moved south, they produced sporadic instances of large hail and wind damage as well as one EF-0 tornado in Mount Sterling. Additional storms in the morning produced an isolated pocket of tree and powerline damage in eastern Vernon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-0 tornado began in a corn field northwest of Mount Sterling with drone footage capturing the swirling corn pattern. The tornado continued southeast damaging a farm outbuilding and a few buildings in town before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved across the southern half of Minnesota into western Wisconsin during the evening hours of September 21st. The storm environment consisted of favorable instability, low cloud bases, and some veering winds with height. Although the shear profile was considered only marginal (weak) at the time, one storm in Wisconsin did manage to become severe. One line of storms developed over south central MN and moved into western St. Croix County where quarter size hail was reported one mile north of Hudson.
An upper level disturbance and the proximity of a stationary front brought repeated thunderstorms with heavy rainfall to south central WI including Portage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of minor street flooding  in the city of Portage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front and upper level disturbance brought scattered thunderstorms to south central WI especially north of Madison. Large hail was reported in Marquette County.

Thunderstorms due to a cold front and upper level disturbance produced large hail and damaging winds to portions of southeast WI. A lightning fatality also occurred to a man on a hiking trail in the Township of Kewaskum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources was struck by lightning when working outside near the New Fane hiking trail. The lightning strike was fatal.
